name: streams
context: Node.js Streams API for efficient data processing with Readable, Writable, Transform, Duplex streams, pipeline, backpressure, and async iteration
info: Node.js streams are an abstraction for working with data that arrives or departs in chunks rather than all at once. There are four fundamental stream types: Readable (data source), Writable (data sink), Transform (read-write with modification), and Duplex (read-write independently). Streams implement backpressure automatically, preventing fast producers from overwhelming slow consumers. They support object mode for non-Buffer payloads, async iteration via `for await...of`, and safe composition through `stream.pipeline()` and `stream.promises.pipeline()`. Streams power nearly every I/O surface in Node.js: HTTP request/response bodies, file system `createReadStream`/`createWriteStream`, zlib compression, crypto ciphers, child process stdio, and TCP/TLS sockets. Mastering streams is essential for building memory-efficient agents that process large datasets, proxy HTTP traffic, or transform data on the fly without buffering entire payloads in memory.
triggers: read a file as a stream
iterate over a stream with for await

sections.title: Readable streams
sections.body: Readable streams are data sources. Create them with `fs.createReadStream()`, `Readable.from()` (from iterables/generators), or by subclassing `Readable` and implementing `_read()`. Key events: `data` (new chunk), `end` (no more data), `error`, `close`. Readable streams support flowing mode (data events fire automatically) and paused mode (must call `.read()` explicitly). Use `.pause()` and `.resume()` to toggle. `highWaterMark` controls the internal buffer size (default 64KB). In modern code, prefer `for await...of` over event listeners. See [[node/fs]] for file-based readable streams.

sections.title: Writable streams
sections.body: Writable streams are data sinks. Create them with `fs.createWriteStream()` or by subclassing `Writable` and implementing `_write()` and optionally `_writev()` (for batch writes) and `_final()` (cleanup). Key events: `drain` (buffer emptied, safe to write more), `finish` (all data flushed after `.end()` called), `error`, `close`. The `.write()` method returns `false` when the internal buffer exceeds `highWaterMark` (default 16KB), signaling backpressure. Always call `.end()` to signal you are done writing. See [[node/fs]] for file-based writable streams.

sections.title: Transform streams
sections.body: Transform streams implement both Readable and Writable interfaces, modifying data as it passes through. Subclass `Transform` and implement `_transform(chunk, encoding, callback)`. Call `this.push(data)` to emit transformed data and `callback()` to signal readiness for the next chunk. Implement `_flush(callback)` to emit any buffered data when the input ends. Use `readableObjectMode: true` and/or `writableObjectMode: true` for mixed-mode transforms (e.g., read bytes, output objects). Common built-in transforms: `zlib.createGzip()`, `zlib.createGunzip()`, `crypto.createCipheriv()`, `crypto.createDecipheriv()`. `PassThrough` is a no-op Transform useful for tapping/monitoring data flow.

sections.title: pipeline() for safe composition
sections.body: Always use `stream.pipeline()` (or `stream.promises.pipeline()` for async/await) instead of `.pipe()`. The `.pipe()` method does not forward errors, does not destroy streams on failure, and can cause memory leaks. `pipeline()` connects a chain of streams, forwards errors, and cleans up all streams if any stream in the chain fails or is destroyed. Signature: `pipeline(source, ...transforms, destination, callback)` or `await pipeline(source, ...transforms, destination)` for the promise version from `stream/promises`. You can also pass async generator functions as transform steps in pipeline.

sections.title: Async iteration with for await...of
sections.body: Every Readable stream implements `Symbol.asyncIterator`, meaning you can consume data with `for await (const chunk of stream)`. This is the most ergonomic way to read streams in modern Node.js. The loop respects backpressure automatically. Combine with `readline.createInterface()` for line-by-line iteration of text streams. When the loop exits (normally or via `break`/`return`), the stream is destroyed automatically. Async iteration works with both Buffer-mode and object-mode streams.

sections.title: Backpressure and flow control
sections.body: Backpressure is the mechanism that prevents a fast Readable from overwhelming a slow Writable. When `.write()` returns `false`, the Writable's buffer is full and the producer should stop sending data until the `drain` event fires. When using `pipeline()` or `.pipe()`, backpressure is handled automatically. When calling `.write()` manually, you must check the return value and pause/resume accordingly. The `highWaterMark` option on both Readable and Writable streams controls the buffer threshold. For object-mode streams, `highWaterMark` is the number of objects (default 16). For Buffer-mode, it is bytes (default 16KB for Writable, 64KB for Readable).

sections.title: Object mode streams
sections.body: By default, streams operate on `Buffer` or `string` data. Set `objectMode: true` to pass arbitrary JavaScript objects through a stream. In object mode, `highWaterMark` represents the number of objects (default 16) rather than bytes. Use `readableObjectMode` and `writableObjectMode` independently on Transform streams for mixed-mode pipelines (e.g., read raw bytes, output parsed objects). Object mode streams are ideal for data processing pipelines where each chunk is a record, event, or message.

sections.title: Web Streams interop
sections.body: Node.js 18+ includes the Web Streams API (`ReadableStream`, `WritableStream`, `TransformStream`). Convert between Node.js and Web streams: `Readable.toWeb(nodeReadable)` converts a Node Readable to a Web ReadableStream, `Readable.fromWeb(webReadable)` does the reverse. Similarly, `Writable.toWeb()` and `Writable.fromWeb()` exist for Writable streams. This interop is essential for using `fetch()` response bodies (which are Web ReadableStreams) in Node.js stream pipelines.

related.note: Understanding the Node.js event loop and Buffer types is foundational for working with streams
File system streams (createReadStream/createWriteStream) are the most common stream usage
Child process stdio (stdin/stdout/stderr) are streams, enabling piped command execution
